content.text: A feature flag without a defined lifecycle is a liability. Flags created during development and never retired accumulate as dead code paths, complicate testing, and erode team confidence in the flag system. The flag lifecycle—from creation through internal testing, beta, canary, general availability, and retirement—is a governance process that ensures every flag has a clear purpose at each stage, defined transition criteria, and an expiry date after which the winning code path becomes unconditional.

The flag lifecycle from development to production is the formal governance process that governs how an agent capability flag is created, advanced through rollout stages, and eventually retired after the capability is fully deployed. The lifecycle has six stages: Created (flag exists, capability is OFF for all users), Internal (capability is ON for the development team only), Beta (ON for a small cohort of external users, typically 1-10%), Canary (ON for a larger percentage, 25-50%), General Availability (ON for 100% of users), and Retired (the flag is removed from code and the service configuration).

Each stage transition has defined gates—observable criteria that must be met before the flag advances. These gates are tailored for agentic AI capabilities: they include evaluation score thresholds, error rate bounds, latency constraints, and security review sign-offs. The retirement stage is mandatory: once a capability reaches 100% rollout, a cleanup ticket is opened to remove the flag within 30 days, preventing the accumulation of flag debt.

Without a defined lifecycle, flags behave like entropy: they start with clear purpose and end as mystery toggles that nobody dares remove because 'something might break'. In agentic AI systems, accumulated flag debt creates a combinatorial explosion of code paths: with 10 flags each with two states, there are 1,024 possible execution paths in the agent. Testing and understanding all paths is intractable.

A flag lifecycle with mandatory retirement gates at each stage ensures flags have a defined end state. The development team commits to a cleanup timeline at creation time, not retroactively. This commitment changes the incentive structure: developers ship the capability, confirm it works, and immediately open the cleanup PR rather than leaving the flag indefinitely.
Controlled exposure risk: each lifecycle stage provides a defined safety checkpoint before expanding the capability's audience.
Mandatory cleanup: the RETIRED stage requirement prevents flag debt from accumulating indefinitely.
Defined rollback points: the lifecycle stages double as rollback targets; at any stage, the flag can be reduced to a previous stage without a code deploy.
Governance trail: the lifecycle stages create an auditable history of who approved each expansion and what criteria were met.
Team coordination: the lifecycle stages provide a shared vocabulary for cross-team discussions about capability readiness.
Predictable release: product and ML teams can predict when a capability will reach general availability based on its current lifecycle stage and stage criteria.
Process overhead: each stage transition requires a formal gate check, adding bureaucracy to the capability development process.
Stage timing variability: the time spent in each stage depends on the quality of evaluation feedback, which can be unpredictable.
Retirement discipline required: teams that skip the retirement stage accumulate technical debt despite having a lifecycle policy.
Multi-team coordination: advancing a flag through stages often requires sign-off from multiple teams (ML, safety, platform), which can create bottlenecks.

The lifecycle is managed through a combination of the flag service configuration and a lightweight tracking mechanism (a ticket, a Google Doc, or a dedicated table in the team wiki). At creation time, the flag entry records: the flag name, the capability it gates, the owner team, the creation date, the planned retirement date, and the success criteria for each stage transition.

Stage transitions are triggered by the owner team after verifying the transition criteria. For the Internal-to-Beta transition, the criteria typically include: a 1-week internal evaluation with no P1 incidents, accuracy and safety scores within tolerance of the stable path, and security team sign-off on any new tools. The flag service is then updated to expand the target population, and the transition is recorded in the tracking mechanism.

attrs.alt: Feature flag lifecycle stages from Created through Retired with transition gates

content.text: The lifecycle architecture flows left to right through six stages. Rollback arrows point backward: at any stage, the flag can be reduced to the previous stage. The retirement stage removes the flag from both the flag service configuration and the codebase. Each stage transition is gated by observable criteria that the flag owner evaluates before advancing. The criteria are calibrated to the risk level of each expansion: internal testing has looser criteria than beta, beta has looser criteria than canary, and canary has the strictest criteria before general availability.
Any flag that controls an agent capability that will serve production users.
Flags that gate capabilities involving new models, new tool sets, or new prompt templates.
Capabilities that have safety implications and require safety team sign-off at specific stages.
Any capability that will be rolled out to external users; internal-only flags may use a simpler lifecycle.
Teams with compliance requirements that mandate documented review at each capability expansion stage.

# scripts/flag-lifecycle-manager.py
# Manages flag lifecycle state and enforces transition gates

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

class FlagStage(Enum):
    CREATED = "created"
    INTERNAL = "internal"
    BETA = "beta"
    CANARY = "canary"
    GENERAL = "general"
    RETIRED = "retired"

@dataclass
class FlagLifecycle:
    flag_name: str
    capability: str
    owner_team: str
    created_at: str
    planned_retirement: str   # ISO date
    current_stage: FlagStage = FlagStage.CREATED
    traffic_pct: float = 0.0
    transitions: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    def advance_stage(self, new_stage: FlagStage, approved_by: str, notes: str = ""):
        """Record a stage transition with approver and timestamp."""
        transition = {
            "from_stage": self.current_stage.value,
            "to_stage": new_stage.value,
            "approved_by": approved_by,
            "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
            "notes": notes,
        }
        self.transitions.append(transition)
        self.current_stage = new_stage
        print(f"Flag '{self.flag_name}' advanced to {new_stage.value} by {approved_by}")

    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "flag_name": self.flag_name,
            "capability": self.capability,
            "owner_team": self.owner_team,
            "created_at": self.created_at,
            "planned_retirement": self.planned_retirement,
            "current_stage": self.current_stage.value,
            "traffic_pct": self.traffic_pct,
            "transitions": self.transitions,
        }

# Flag registry: tracks all active flags and their lifecycle state
class FlagRegistry:
    REGISTRY_FILE = ".flags/lifecycle-registry.json"

    def __init__(self):
        self._flags: dict[str, FlagLifecycle] = {}
        self._load()

    def _load(self):
        path = Path(self.REGISTRY_FILE)
        if path.exists():
            data = json.loads(path.read_text())
            for name, entry in data.items():
                f = FlagLifecycle(**entry)
                f.current_stage = FlagStage(entry["current_stage"])
                self._flags[name] = f

    def save(self):
        Path(self.REGISTRY_FILE).parent.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
        Path(self.REGISTRY_FILE).write_text(
            json.dumps({k: v.to_dict() for k, v in self._flags.items()}, indent=2)
        )

    def create_flag(self, name: str, capability: str, owner: str, retirement_days: int = 90):
        """Create a new flag in the CREATED stage."""
        created_at = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
        planned = (datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(days=retirement_days)).date().isoformat()
        self._flags[name] = FlagLifecycle(
            flag_name=name,
            capability=capability,
            owner_team=owner,
            created_at=created_at,
            planned_retirement=planned,
        )
        self.save()
        print(f"Flag '{name}' created. Planned retirement: {planned}")

    def check_for_overdue_retirements(self) -> list[str]:
        """Return flags past their planned retirement date."""
        today = datetime.utcnow().date()
        overdue = []
        for name, flag in self._flags.items():
            if flag.current_stage == FlagStage.GENERAL:
                retirement = datetime.fromisoformat(flag.planned_retirement).date()
                if today > retirement:
                    overdue.append(name)
        return overdue

registry = FlagRegistry()
# Example usage:
# registry.create_flag("agent-browser-tool", "Browser capability for agent", "platform-team")
# registry.save()
overdue = registry.check_for_overdue_retirements()
if overdue:
    print(f"WARNING: {len(overdue)} flag(s) are overdue for retirement: {overdue}")

# .flags/agent-browser-tool.yaml
# Flag lifecycle definition for the browser tool capability

flag_name: agent-browser-tool
capability: "Browser tool access for the main agent"
owner_team: platform-team
created_date: "2026-07-01"
planned_retirement: "2026-09-30"  # 90 days after creation

stage_criteria:
  internal:
    description: "Enable for @platform-team and @ml-team members"
    target: "users with internal=true attribute"
    min_duration_days: 7
    requirements:
      - "Kill switch tested in staging"
      - "Both flag-on and flag-off paths covered by unit tests"
      - "Evaluation baseline recorded"
      
  beta:
    description: "Enable for 5% of production users"
    target: "5% percentage rollout"
    min_duration_days: 14
    requirements:
      - "Internal eval: accuracy delta < 1%"
      - "No P1 incidents from internal use"
      - "Security team review completed"
      - "Browser tool sandboxing reviewed"
      
  canary:
    description: "Enable for 25% then 50%"
    target: "percentage rollout"
    min_duration_days: 7
    requirements:
      - "Beta eval gate passed (regression < 1%)"
      - "Error rate < 0.1% on flagged traffic"
      - "Latency p99 < 2x stable path"
      
  general:
    description: "Enable for 100% of users"
    requirements:
      - "Canary metrics stable for 7 days"
      - "No open P1 or P2 incidents"
      - "Cleanup ticket created in Jira"
      
  retired:
    description: "Flag removed from code and flag service"
    deadline_days_after_general: 30
    requirements:
      - "PR merged removing flag-off code path"
      - "Flag removed from LaunchDarkly"
      - "Changelog entry: feat: browser tool now generally available"

transitions:
  - from: created
    to: internal
    approved_by: alice@company.com
    date: "2026-07-08"
  - from: internal
    to: beta
    approved_by: bob@company.com
    date: "2026-07-22"
    notes: "Internal eval passed. No P1 incidents. Security approved."
current_stage: beta
Lifecycle transitions for a new multi-hop reasoning capability:
Day 0 (CREATED): Flag 'agent-multihop' created, OFF for all users. Cleanup deadline set to Day 90.
Day 7 (INTERNAL): Kill switch tested. Both paths covered by tests. Eval baseline recorded. Flag advanced to INTERNAL for engineering team.
Day 14 (BETA): 7 days of internal testing. Accuracy delta: +2.1% (improvement). No P1 incidents. Safety reviewed. Flag advanced to BETA at 5%.
Day 28 (CANARY): 2 weeks of beta. Regression suite passed. Error rate 0.08%. Latency p99 +120ms (acceptable). Flag advanced to CANARY at 25%, then 50%.
Day 42 (GENERAL): Canary stable for 7 days. No open P1/P2. Flag advanced to 100%. Cleanup ticket created.
Day 65 (RETIRED): Cleanup PR merged. Flag-off code path removed. Flag deleted from LaunchDarkly.

A customer service AI team introduced a lifecycle policy after discovering they had 23 active feature flags in their agent codebase, 14 of which were at 100% rollout but had never been retired. Their CI test matrix was notionally 2^23 paths, with active coverage of only a few. A new engineer introduced a bug in a code path that was only active when two specific flags were both ON—a combination nobody had thought to test.

After the incident, the team implemented a flag lifecycle registry and a weekly CI check that failed if any flag had been at GENERAL stage for more than 30 days without a retirement PR. Within 8 weeks, the 14 overdue flags were retired, reducing the active flag count to 9. The team reported that code was 'noticeably cleaner' and that new engineers understood the codebase faster with fewer conditional paths to reason about.
flags without a planned retirement date are never retired. Set the deadline at creation time.
skipping the internal or beta stage removes the safety checkpoints that catch behavioral regressions before they affect a wide audience.
a lifecycle registry that is not kept current becomes inaccurate and loses team trust. Automate stage recording where possible.
Retiring the flag without removing the code:
removing a flag from the flag service without also removing the flag-off code path leaves dead code that confuses future developers.
Define the stage criteria and retirement deadline before creating a flag, not retrospectively.
Automate the overdue retirement check in weekly CI to prevent flags from lingering at GENERAL stage.
Require cleanup PRs to remove the flag-off code path within 30 days of reaching GENERAL stage.
Document each stage transition in the lifecycle registry with approver, date, and evaluation evidence.
Use the flag service's built-in expiry feature (if available) as a backstop that disables the flag if retirement is not done on time.
Review all active flags in the quarterly engineering offsite to identify any that can be expedited to retirement.

The lifecycle policy itself has no runtime performance impact—it is a governance process, not a code path. The flag evaluation at request time is the same regardless of lifecycle stage. The main scalability concern is the flag registry: a team with many flags needs a searchable registry, not a YAML file. Consider using a feature flag management tool (LaunchDarkly, Unleash) that provides lifecycle tracking as a built-in feature.

The flag lifecycle is a security control for agentic AI systems. Requiring security team sign-off at the Internal-to-Beta transition ensures that new capabilities (especially those involving new tools that make external calls) are reviewed before they reach external users. The lifecycle registry provides an audit trail that compliance teams can use to demonstrate that each capability expansion was reviewed and approved.

Reliability is maintained through the mandatory kill switch test at the CREATED-to-INTERNAL transition. Before any user sees a new capability, the team verifies that turning the flag off works correctly—including in edge cases like in-flight agent sessions.
The flag lifecycle has six stages: Created, Internal, Beta, Canary, General, Retired—each with defined transition criteria.
Set the planned retirement date at flag creation time to prevent indefinite accumulation of flag debt.
Require cleanup PRs to remove flag-off code paths within 30 days of reaching general availability.
Gate each stage transition with observable criteria: eval scores, error rates, latency bounds, and security review.
Automate overdue retirement detection in weekly CI to enforce the retirement deadline.
Document each transition in the lifecycle registry with approver, date, and supporting evaluation evidence.
